comment.body:
the best way to differenciate between vomma and vanna impacts is.. that to know that vvol is the kurtosis of the distribution. It doesn't have a direction, it only says that once the vol strikes, how big magnitude it will have...
while vanna is the skewness itself... it assigns a 'direction' for the vol impact... it tells you if the market is negatively skewed (left-tail risk), or positively (right tail risk)
skewness shifts slowly, while kurtosis changes more...
vomma is nearer-term, vanna is medium-term
comment.raw_body:
the best way to differenciate between vomma and vanna impacts is.. that to know that vvol is the kurtosis of the distribution. It doesn't have a direction, it only says that once the vol strikes, how big magnitude it will have...
while vanna is the skewness itself... it assigns a 'direction' for the vol impact... it tells you if the market is negatively skewed (left-tail risk), or positively (right tail risk)
skewness shifts slowly, while kurtosis changes more...
vomma is nearer-term, vanna is medium-term

comment.body:
i tell you why...
lets say you buy 10 call option with delta of 60, and lets say, spot goes against you, and your position's delta will be only 45. you sell 7 call options. Nominally you sold 70% of your position, but notionally you still have 47.5% of it.
Why? bcs when you bough 10 calls with 60 deltas, its like you longed 600 shares of stocks (10 * 60), price went against you, your delta is now 45, you sell 7 calls, is like basically selling 7 * 45 = 315 shares of stocks.
600 - 315 = 285
you still have 285 shares of stock mathematically
comment.raw_body:
